bibliographic_data.title: Artificial Intelligence Fairness in the Context of Accessibility Research on Intelligent Systems for People who are Deaf or Hard of Hearing
bibliographic_data.abstract: We discuss issues of Artificial Intelligence (AI) fairness for people with disabilities, with examples drawn from our research on HCI for AI-based systems for people who are Deaf or Hard of Hearing (DHH). In particular, we discuss the need for inclusion of data from people with disabilities in training sets, the lack of interpretability of AI systems, ethical responsibilities of access technology researchers and companies, the need for appropriate evaluation metrics for AI-based access technologies (to determine if they are ready to be deployed and if they can be trusted by users), and the ways in which AI systems influence human behavior and influence the set of abilities needed by users to successfully interact with computing systems.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper discusses issues related to AI fairness for people with disabilities, particularly focusing on Deaf or Hard of Hearing (DHH) individuals and the ethical implications of deploying AI-based access technologies.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors draw examples from their prior research in human-computer interaction and computing accessibility, specifically on AI-powered systems for DHH users.
outcomes: Highlighting the need for diverse training data to ensure fairness in AI systems
Emphasizing the lack of interpretability in modern AI-based technologies and its ethical implications
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper discusses issues related to Artificial Intelligence (AI) fairness for people with disabilities, particularly focusing on Deaf or Hard of Hearing (DHH) individuals. The authors highlight the need for diverse training data to ensure that AI systems perform well across different user groups and the ethical responsibilities of researchers and companies in deploying these technologies. They also discuss the lack of interpretability in modern AI-based systems and its implications, especially for marginalized users who may not have advanced computing training.
